speaker: interviewer
text: --nee Rychter. We left off, you were telling us about your train journey back to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Czestochowa.

speaker: interviewee
text: So as I said, the moment they-- they said, 'Jew,' I-- I felt trapped. And I did not react anymore. And the atmosphere was getting less friendly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at that moment, the train conductor came to check the tickets. He was a tall older man. He probably was not older, but this is the way he looked to me at that time. He's sized up the situation quickly, and he asked for the tickets in a very all official way and asked, what's going on here?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then one of these women said, oh, we have this-- this little Jewish girl who is trying to escape the Kikes from the Warsaw. And this man looked at them [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and he told them, in so many words, that they have to mind their own business and that he knows quite a few things about them, too. You see, they were smugglers, so they were vulnerable. And they knew that there are policemen and Germans. Smuggling was punished. I cannot tell you how I felt about this man who really saved my life. I never saw him again, but I remember exactly how he looked. And-- and I only spent one day outside of the ghetto during the war. Living outside of the ghetto was terrible. Hundreds of people I paid with their deaths for, you know, being Jewish. People were sold sometimes for a kilo of sugar or, a bottle of vodka. However, everyone who did survive on the other side of the ghetto, including myself during this one day, survived because people helped him. So this has to be remembered.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were good Poles, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And in my case, completely unknown to-- completely not friends, nobody, who knew me, just decent people. And so I came to Czestochowa. I went straight into to ghetto. I don't remember how I made my way, but I knew the place very well and the ghetto. I went to live with my-- to stay you with one of my uncles. He was the one where my grandmother lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Manche. His name was Manche. He was younger than my father. He was a tailor, and he was the-- there were three of them in the ghetto, in the Czestochowa ghetto. Two were very, very poor. Very nice people. With families, everybody. This one was a little bit better off, and my grandmother lived with him.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this is where I was. I met my friends from school. It was much, much quieter and better than-- than the Warsaw ghetto. The Warsaw ghetto was really terrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Czestochowa ghetto, at that point, of course, you know, they were also rounding people, sending people out, killing. But there was no comparison. There was still some life going on, and the hunger certainly was not as terrible as that in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a fenced ghetto in Czestochowa?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it wasn't fenced. It-- it wasn't walled. It was fenced, but not walled.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were-- who-- who were-- was in charge of the Czestochowa ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there was Judenrat, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you know any--

speaker: interviewee
text: I know very little about them. No. I know very-- there was Jewish police. The organization was the same. Yeah. But, of course, the population was smaller. The hunger was not that bad. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you knew people?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I knew people, yes. I was not as isolated as I had been in the Warsaw ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were-- was the ghetto one large ghetto or what was the--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the large ghetto. This was the first. The large ghetto-- the liquidation of the ghetto did not come yet at that point. It came in 1942, both in Warsaw and here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no. At the beginning, I didn't do anything special. I tried to join some of my friends to get some schooling, but they were advanced by two years. So I gave it up. And pretty soon my brother came. My brother, as you know, was in Bialystok with my father. He matriculated there. He enrolled in a medical school in Lwow. And he was supposed to start in 1941, but he didn't. Now, together with my father, they started to escape from Bialystok. But they were late. My father was the editor of a newspaper. He was a-- a-- an eternal optimist. He never believed there's going to be a war. It was the same in 1939. The same in 1941. He wanted to get his paper ready.

speaker: interviewee
text: So many of his friends managed to-- to go, to run. And he, with my brother, were too late. They were overrun by the Germans. And they lost themselves. This was close to the city, to the town of Slonim. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother didn't know what happened. And I'll never know how and exactly when my father died, or was killed. Probably it happened in Slonim, where there were two massacres, large massacres, right in the beginning of the German rule. One--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was when?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was when?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- the starting-- they came to Slonim a few days after the outbreak of the war in June, 1941. And the Slonim massacres were one in 19-- in July, '41. The other one in November, '41. But he may have been killed on the walls-- on the road. He may have-- oh, you know, there's no end to imagine how he died. And how he was tormented. But I'll never know it. And my brother started to walk back to Czestochowa. Somehow, he-- maybe he thought that we may be in Czestochowa rather than in Warsaw. He was then 18 [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] or 17. And he walked all the way. And this is far away. This is from the northeastern part of the-- of Poland to the southwestern part of Poland. He avoided big cities. And was walking through villages, stopping there to do some work, being fed. Of course, you know, he never disclosed his Jewish origin. And finally, he came. He came to Czestochowa sometimes in October, or maybe it was November, 1941. So the two of us were together, and pretty soon my mother joined us, too. She also escaped the ghetto. And, again, either I don't remember, or I didn't ask her, I don't know the details of-- of her escape. I would--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that feeling like to be reunited with your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a good feeling. But unfortunately, I don't remember very much, or I never asked him any details of his life with my father, his life in-- in the Russian part of Poland. I remember only a few Russian songs that she-- or that he was singing. These were the first Russian songs I heard, and I liked them very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to sing one now?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't think I will.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my mother came. There were the three of us. Again, there was no money.

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in the kitchen of-- of my youngest uncle's apartment. It was a room and a very small, narrow kitchen. And this is where we settled. This young man, we called him [? Romek. ?] His Jewish name was [? Reuven, ?] was-- had a-- was a tailor also. And he had a wife with some type of congenital heart disease, And At that time, she was bedridden. She just couldn't walk. They had a little girl, a four-year-old child. He gave us the kitchen, and we-- we were there. My brother went to work. He started to work in so-called mobel lager, which was a large warehouse of furniture run by the Germans. And I understand that they were taking Jewish furniture, renewing them, collecting, repairing them, and sending them off to-- to Germany. This was very hard work. He was coming home very, very tired. I remember once seeing-- in this small kitchen, we-- there was only a place for one bed, and then a cot. It was put up only in the evening for him. And around 8 o'clock, after eating our soup-- or maybe it was 7 o'clock in the evening-- we all went to bed. And then a friend of Henryk came just to visit with his school friend. And he was from a rich family, a very nice boy. And he looked at-- in this scene, then he sat down on my brother's cot and asked him to whistle something for him, because Henryk whistled beautifully. And I remember this whistling. He whistled the 'Eine Kleine Nachtmuzik' by Mozart. And we all cried. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Well, we were poor. I started to do something, and I don't remember exactly what, but I remember cleaning some place where children played during the day. It was a sort of a communal kindergarten or something. So I would wash the floors there, but I don't remember very much of that. Well, this is how life was going on until September, 1942. And September, 1942, on the 22nd of September, the so-called Big Action started. But on the 21st of September, my brother came from work and told us to come with him. Apparently there was this German, his name was Lange, who was their supervisor. There were a group of young people, strong people, working there. And he told them, tomorrow the ghetto will start being liquidated. The Big Action is started, and the Ukrainian and the [INAUDIBLE] troops are already in. And one night, they are going to surround the ghetto. And if you want, bring your closest family, and they'll be hidden here. This is what I know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went. What did you take with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Literally nothing. We went as we stood, because he was rushing us because it was close to the curfew. I don't even know how we went, because we had to go out of the ghetto. So it had to be somehow organized. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you not take any food with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food? No. No, we didn't. But we were fed there. And so we went. We were put up in an attic. Actually, it wasn't an attic, really, because it was-- there was-- there were no stairs to go there . It was directly under the roof. Sort of that provisional bunker. And I don't know how many we were. About-- anything between 50 to a hundred. I don't know. Apparently there was another bunker in the cellar with families. We stayed there at least two weeks, if not more. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this some distance from the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was. But it was not very far.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you hear from your hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what I heard was the selections that were being done over there. I mean, these boys lived there. They-- everybody who worked on this type of-- in this type of a German-run place was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know the word in English. [SPEAKING POLISH] They stayed there. And the rest of the people in the ghetto started to be-- nice-- nice word for it is deported. But, you know, I don't know what the word is or anything

speaker: interviewer
text: The Polish word you used, could that be Labor Brigade?

speaker: interviewee
text: Could that be?

speaker: interviewer
text: That Polish word you used?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Could that be labeled? No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Labor. Labor Brigade? Labor Unit?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING POLISH] mean that they were-- they lived there. Like-- like the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Inmates?

speaker: interviewee
text: --military lives in-- live in barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: So these boys, and all the workers, all the Jewish workers, stayed there. We were in this attic and in the cellar being hidden. And the Germans, of course, would do their business, sending transport after transport of, you know, 50 or so thousand Jews to Treblinka, and killing many on the spot.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we didn't go through it. However, the Germans came to these places, and they were making these [NON-ENGLISH] selection, it was called, over there. So what we heard was the screams of the Germans from-- from the yard. And we never knew who was taken and who wasn't. But what was most important was that we had to be absolutely quiet, particularly during these selections on the courtyard. And there were some children there. So the children were [SIGHS] it was not always possible to keep them completely quiet. And I only remember that one child was suffocated by a pillow. Just his mouth was covered with a pillow. It was a young child who wouldn't stop crying. And after it all ended, the child was dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you-- who fed you while you were staying in the mobellager?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who stayed?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who fed you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The mobellager. This same German, and our boys. They would come in the evening through a opening in the-- masked opening in the-- in the ceiling. They would bring us food, and they would take our-- our buckets down. And we were-- I was better fed there than in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you remain in hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it must have been two-- more than two weeks, because the actions started in-- on the 22nd of September, 1942. And they ended around 8 or so of October. So sometimes in the middle of October, when the Small Ghetto was already organized, we were smuggled in into this Small Ghetto. And we were not the only one, because there were people in bunkers, prepared bunkers. You know, all types of people. So they came back. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people do you estimate were in the large ghetto before the deportations began? Approximately.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, I told you before that there were 30,000 Jews, approximately, in Warsaw. In addition--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Czestochowa?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Czestochowa. In addition, there were people from the neighboring little towns. And I believe that the total number was close to 45,000, 50,000. And what was left in the Small Ghetto was 5,000 people. Everybody else went. Out of my three uncles with their families, only one, Manche, the first one that I stayed with, was still alive. His family, of course, my grandmother and his wife and the child, and the other two families, entire families were-- perished. Perished there. They were transported to Treblinka, and many of them killed on the spot.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can imagine that my grandmother who is older-- The elderly. The young.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And this young woman with heart disease who couldn't get out of bed, you know, for sure. But, again, I will never know.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to ask you what would be the reason for them keeping a labor force behind in a Small Ghetto? If you could tell us about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they went after their business in a very systematic way. They still-- they wanted to exterminate, and the Final Solution was a Final Solution-- all the Jews. But as long as some Jews were useful-- useful, working, doing something, you know, they kept them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were the remaining Jews employed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were all types of places. There was a place that later on became my camp, my concentration camp. And it was an ammunition or munition factory. Large munition factory. Then there was a steel mill, Rakow. And there were places like this mobellager. And some other places.

speaker: interviewer
text: The factory at Rakow, and the one that you were employed in later on, were they-- did they belong to an organization as such?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yeah. Before the war, this ammunition factory used to be a textile factory place owned by French people. And then when the Germans came, they rebuilt the whole place. They needed a munition factory. They brought all the machines and equipment. And this is-- and there were some other places. Czestochowa was a fairly industrial place, so this is where these people worked.

speaker: interviewee
text: So 5,000 or so of us were left in the Small Ghetto. The Small Ghetto was a horror. The Small Ghetto, we were like hunted animals. You went to work in the morning-- they would take us in groups to different places-- and we would come back in the evening. And you never know who is going to come back or who is going to be left, who you are going to find there.

speaker: interviewee
text: But now I want to talk about my brother. Very soon after the organization of the Small Ghetto, my brother came to-- to my mother and myself, and he told us that he's joining a partisan group. There were six of them, six young boys I know-- I knew, and they already had some guns. Later on I learned that there was a unit of the-- a job at the Jewish fighting organization in Czestochowa, and this was most probably part of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he went. This must have been around November or December of 1942. They went. Well, I don't know. Should I-- should I finish his story or should I go in a chronological way? I think let's talk about your brother and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We didn't hear from him for a long time, but then in March, and this was around the 17th of March of 1943, my mother got a message. The message said that they are in the city now-- they came to the city-- and he wants to talk with her and to see her. And he is going to be in a bunker together with these other boys in mobellager. And when she goes to work, her group was passing by there, it was arranged with the kapo that on the way back from work, she would join her son in the bunker and spend the night with them. And then in the morning, she would be picked up by her group again to go to work, so nobody would notice that she's not at work. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] At that time, we had an idea or at least-- this is what it seems to me-- that the boys had some problems joining a group of partisans. There was the-- the Polish underground was divided. There was the mainstream, the Home Army, the Armia Krajowa; there was the left, the Armia Ludowa, the False Army; and there were some very right-wing group, some of them extremely virulent and very, very anti-Semitic who actually fought-- fought with the Germans and with the Jews. And the idea was that they may have some problems finding the-- the AL, which is the Volksarmee that did accept Jews. But I didn't know, and I still don't know exactly what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the next thing I knew, on the 18th of March, I came back from work. This was the night when my mother was supposed to-- to be-- to join my brother.
